title: Union Find / Disjoint Set
description: A different kind of tool entirely: quickly answering 'are these two vertices in the same group?' — with path compression, and cycle detection as its classic use.
objectives: Understand the problem union find solves: tracking disjoint groups
Implement union() to merge two groups
Use union find to detect a cycle in an undirected graph

sections.text: 1. Recap: A New Kind of Question

BFS and DFS answer "how do I reach every vertex from here?" Union find answers a different, narrower question extremely efficiently: "are these two specific vertices already connected, directly or indirectly?"
Union find is a data structure that tracks a collection of disjoint (non-overlapping) groups, supporting two operations: find (which group is this element in?) and union (merge two groups into one).
Each element points to a "parent" — following parent pointers upward eventually reaches a root, which represents that entire group. Initially, everyone is their own root, meaning n separate groups.
Path compression makes every node visited during a find() point directly to the root, so future find() calls on those same nodes become nearly instant.
5. Full Example: union() and Cycle Detection

sections.title: Why the last edge is flagged
points: After processing (0,1) and (1,2), vertices 0, 1, and 2 all belong to the SAME group (connected, directly or indirectly)
(3,4) forms a completely separate second group
When (0,2) is processed, find(0) and find(2) return the SAME root — they're already connected
Adding an edge between two vertices already in the same group would create a cycle, exactly what unite() correctly detects and reports

sections.title: Merging friend groups, and noticing when two people are already in the same one
sections.text: Imagine tracking separate friend groups as people get introduced to each other. Introducing two people from entirely different groups merges those groups into one. But if someone tries to "introduce" two people who turn out to already be in the same friend group (through some other chain of mutual friends), that's not a new connection at all — it's redundant, exactly like a cycle in a graph.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting path compression — find() still works without it, but degrades toward O(n) per call on a long chain instead of staying nearly O(1).
Comparing a and b directly instead of comparing find(a) and find(b) — two elements in the same group don't necessarily have the same immediate parent, only the same ROOT.
Assuming union find can answer 'what is the shortest path between these two' — it can only answer 'are they connected at all,' nothing about the path itself.
Forgetting to initialize parent[i] = i for every element before use — an uninitialized parent array doesn't represent 'everyone is their own group' correctly.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What problem does union find solve?
questions.answer: Efficiently tracking a collection of disjoint groups, answering 'are these two elements in the same group' and merging two groups together.

questions.question: What does path compression do in find()?
questions.answer: It makes every node visited during the find() call point directly to the root, so future find() calls on those nodes become nearly instant.

questions.question: How can union find be used to detect a cycle in an undirected graph?
questions.answer: Process each edge with union(); if the two vertices already share the same root (are already connected) before the edge is added, that edge would create a cycle.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Union find tracks disjoint groups using a parent array, where each element points toward a root representing its group. find(x) follows parent pointers up to the root, applying path compression along the way so future calls become nearly instant. union(a, b) merges two groups by connecting their roots — but if find(a) and find(b) already return the same root, they're already connected, and forcing that edge would create a cycle, which is exactly how union find is used to detect cycles while building an undirected graph edge by edge.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Union Find
sections.text: Union find = merging friend groups. find() = which group's root am I part of? union() = merge two groups. Same root already? That new edge is a cycle.

sections.title: Number of Provinces
sections.problemStatement: Given an n x n adjacency matrix isConnected, return the number of provinces (connected groups of cities).
hints: This lesson's own technique, applied directly: union() every pair of directly-connected cities, then count how many distinct roots remain.
No traversal needed at all — just iterate the matrix once, calling union(i, j) wherever isConnected[i][j] == 1.
The final province count is the number of cities i where find(i) == i (i.e., i is its own root).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Union find WITHOUT path compression or union by rank — find() walks the raw parent chain every time.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n^2 * n) worst case without path compression — a degenerate chain makes find() O(n)

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Union find with path compression — find() flattens the chain directly to the root, making future calls near-instant.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n^2 * α(n)) — near-constant find() amortized with path compression

sections.title: Redundant Connection
sections.problemStatement: A tree with n nodes had one extra edge added, creating exactly one cycle. Given the edges in input order, return the edge that can be removed to make it a tree again — if multiple answers exist, return the one that appears LAST in the input.
hints: This is exactly this lesson's cycle-detection use case: process edges in order, union() each one, and the first edge where find(a) == find(b) BEFORE unioning is the answer.
'Last edge to appear in the input' falls out naturally — just process edges in their given order and return the FIRST one union() reports as a cycle (there's only one, by the problem's guarantee).
No traversal is needed at all — this is purely find()/union() calls in a single pass, no BFS/DFS.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Union find without path compression — find() walks the raw chain, which can degrade on a long unlucky merge order.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n^2) worst case without path compression

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Union find with path compression — near-constant find() per edge.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * α(n)) — near-constant find() amortized with path compression

sections.title: Find if Path Exists in Graph
sections.problemStatement: Given an undirected graph as a list of edges, and two vertices source and destination, return true if there is a path between them.
hints: Instead of BFS/DFS, union() every edge first, then just compare find(source) and find(destination) — same root means connected.
This trades traversal for the union find structure this lesson builds — a clean 'are these two connected at all' fit, with no need to reconstruct a path.
For a single static query like this, BFS/DFS work just as well; union find shines more when checking MANY connectivity queries against a fixed or growing edge set.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.timeComplexity: O(edges * n) worst case without path compression

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.timeComplexity: O(edges * α(n)) — near-constant find() amortized with path compression
